I wanted to believe everything was okay. It makes me feel so foolish now, like I was just ignoring the reality because I didn’t want to face it.

patient.query: I guess it was easier to think that way. I wanted to believe that it was just a phase, that our relationship was strong enough to get through anything. I kept telling myself that we were in it together, that we had each other’s backs. I didn’t want to confront the idea that we might be unraveling. It felt safer to just push those thoughts away.

patient.query: I think I just really wanted to hold on to the good memories, you know? It felt like if I kept believing in us, then everything would work out, like I could will it all to be okay. I guess I was scared of what it would mean if I admitted there were problems. Letting go seemed so daunting, and clinging to hope felt like my only option.

patient.query: Yeah, it really was. It’s like, if I admitted there were problems, it meant facing the possibility that we might not be meant to be. That felt like a huge loss—not just of him but of everything I pictured for my future. I thought about how it would impact my life, my friends, and even my own sense of self. I didn’t want to accept that it might not end happily ever after.

patient.query: It’s just... I always believed in happily ever afters, you know? Growing up, I was surrounded by stories where everything works out in the end. Admitting that it’s not meant to be feels like I’m letting go of that dream. Plus, there’s this fear that if we weren’t meant to be, then what does that say about me? Was I not worthy of love? It just shakes everything I thought I knew about myself and what I wanted.

triggers: admitting it's not meant to be

It’s hard not to take it personally when I invested so much of myself.
